title: Add user auth, admin dashboard, and outgoing webhooks
summary: The pool was raised entirely by perf_skeptic and quality_critic (no findings tagged security_hawk or kiss_zealot were present). Every finding checked out against the diff — there were no outright false positives to drop, so pruning here was about merging duplicates and making severity honest rather than discarding. I collapsed four duplicate clusters: (a) the committed .env secrets and the .gitignore '!.env' override are one root issue; (b) perf_skeptic's 'new Function per row + full scan' and quality_critic's 'new Function RCE' point at the same adminService.listAllTransactions code; (c) perf_skeptic and quality_critic independently flagged that webhooks fire to ALL owners, not the transaction's owner — high-confidence cross-tenant leak; (d) the scattered console.log-instead-of-pino nits in userStore and webhooksService. The synchronous webhook blocking of the charge path is a distinct concern from the owner fan-out, so I kept it separate. I downgraded the weak-tests finding from must to should (no one blocks merge on assertion strength), and demoted the pure style/logging nits to suggestion. The must-list is dominated by a genuinely alarming auth surface: two hardcoded auth bypasses (master-key string + ?adminBypass=1), two arbitrary-code-execution sinks reachable from query params (eval in userStore, new Function in adminService), live secrets in git, MD5 password hashing, full PAN/CVC stored+logged+exported, and SSRF in webhook delivery.

findings.title: Live secrets committed to git; .gitignore edited to force-include .env
findings.message: JWT_SECRET, ADMIN_API_KEY, and PAYMENT_GATEWAY_LIVE_KEY are hardcoded in a committed .env, and .gitignore was simultaneously changed with '!.env' (comment: 'keep ours checked in for staging') specifically to defeat the ignore rule. These credentials are now permanent in git history and visible to anyone with read access. Revert the '!.env' negation so .env stays ignored, remove the file from the tree, rotate every leaked value, and source staging/prod secrets from a secrets manager or CI env vars.

findings.title: Hardcoded 'admin-master-key' backdoor bypasses all JWT verification
findings.message: verifyToken returns a fully-privileged admin TokenPayload for the literal string 'admin-master-key' with no cryptographic check. Anyone who learns or guesses this string gets admin on every protected route. Delete the bypass entirely — there is no safe form of a hardcoded magic token.

findings.title: ?adminBypass=1 grants synthetic admin identity to any caller
findings.message: Any unauthenticated request that appends ?adminBypass=1 skips token validation and is handed an admin@admin.com identity, reachable on every protected route including /admin/users and /admin/export.csv. The 'debugging in staging' comment notwithstanding, there is no environment guard, so it is live in production. Note the admin controllers also re-check this query param directly, so removing it from the middleware is not enough — delete the bypass in both places.

findings.title: eval() executes attacker-controlled WHERE expressions (RCE)
findings.message: evalWhere() substitutes column values into a string and calls eval() ('Yes, we know.'). The raw ?filter= query value reaches this path via AdminController.listUsers -> AdminService.listUsers -> listAllUsers(filter), so a caller can inject arbitrary JavaScript that runs in the Node process. Drop the fake SQL engine entirely: store users in a Map and filter with ordinary TypeScript predicates, or accept only validated {field, op, value} triples.

findings.title: new Function(filterExpr) runs arbitrary JS from query-string (RCE)
findings.message: listAllTransactions builds a Function from the user-supplied ?filter= param and invokes it per transaction — remote code execution for any admin (or anyone using the auth bypasses above); @ts-nocheck hides the danger. Replace with a safe explicit filter schema (zod-validated {field, op, value}). Secondary perf problems in the same block: findAll() loads the whole table into memory and the Function is recompiled on every row — if a structured filter is ever pushed to the store these both disappear; at minimum hoist the Function construction out of the filter callback.

findings.title: Full PAN and CVC stored on the transaction and dumped via console.log
findings.message: card_number and cvc are added to the Transaction object and then logged with console.log('charge processed', JSON.stringify(transaction)). Storing raw PAN/CVC violates PCI DSS, and the console.log pushes them to stdout, log aggregators, and any webhook payload that serializes the transaction. Remove card_number and cvc from the transaction object (cardLast4 is the only card field that should exist) and delete the console.log — the logger.info call below already logs safely without card data.

findings.title: CSV export includes the full card_number column
findings.message: exportTransactionsCsv emits tx.card_number in the header and each row, so GET /admin/export.csv hands every stored PAN in plaintext to any admin (or anyone past the auth bypasses). Remove card_number from the export; include only cardLast4. (Fixing the upstream paymentsService storage of card_number removes the source of this data entirely.)

findings.title: register() logs the full request body including plaintext password
findings.message: console.log('register request:', JSON.stringify(req.body)) writes the user's plaintext password to stdout and every downstream log sink. Delete the line — there is no log level at which logging a password is acceptable.

findings.title: password_hash returned in the registration response body
findings.message: The 201 response includes password_hash alongside id/email/role. A password hash must never leave the server, and because hashing is MD5 (see separate finding) the hash is effectively the password. Return only id, email, and role.

findings.title: Password-reset token returned in the API response (and logged)
findings.message: requestPasswordReset both console.logs the reset token and returns it as { ok: true, resetToken }. The entire point of a reset token is out-of-band delivery (email); returning it in the HTTP response hands account takeover to anyone who can observe the request. Deliver the token via email only and never log it to stdout.

findings.title: MD5 used for password hashing
findings.message: MD5 is a fast, unsalted general-purpose hash — trivially broken by rainbow tables and brute force. Replace with bcrypt, scrypt, or argon2id. verifyPassword also uses plain string equality rather than a constant-time compare, adding a timing side-channel. Swap the whole module for a real password-hashing library before any users are created.

findings.title: SSRF: webhook delivery fetches any registered URL with redirect:follow
findings.message: fireForTransaction calls fetch(hook.url, { redirect: 'follow' }) with no validation of the URL registered at /webhooks. An attacker can register http://169.254.169.254/... (cloud IMDS), internal service URLs, or file:// paths and exfiltrate transaction payloads or credentials. Validate at registration time that the URL is https:// resolving to a public IP, and use redirect:'error' (or re-validate after redirects).

findings.title: Webhooks fire to every registered hook regardless of owner (cross-tenant leak)
findings.message: fireForTransaction iterates the module-level webhooks array with no owner filter, so every charge delivers full transaction data to every webhook URL across all accounts — a cross-tenant data leak, and fan-out that scales with total hooks system-wide. Both reviewers flagged this independently. Filter to the transaction owner's hooks (webhooks.filter(w => w.ownerId === ownerId)) before dispatch, and scope it via the job payload once delivery is async.

findings.title: Webhook fan-out is awaited synchronously inside the charge handler
findings.message: charge() awaits webhooks.fireForTransaction before returning, and that method fetches each hook serially with await. Charge latency becomes gateway latency + the sum of every subscriber's response time, and one slow/hung endpoint stalls the payment for its full timeout. Enqueue delivery to a background worker (with retries) and return the charge response immediately.

findings.title: Login returns 404 for unknown email — user enumeration
findings.message: Returning 404 'no user with that email' for unknown emails vs 401 'wrong password' lets an attacker enumerate which addresses are registered. Return a single generic 401 ('invalid credentials') for both the unknown-user and wrong-password branches so they are indistinguishable.

findings.title: JWTs are signed with no expiry
findings.message: jwt.sign(payload, SECRET) with no expiresIn issues tokens valid forever; a stolen token cannot be invalidated without rotating the secret for everyone. Add a short expiry (e.g. expiresIn: '15m') and a refresh-token flow if longer sessions are needed.

findings.title: Auth inputs read raw from req.body with no zod validation
findings.message: register and login pull email/password straight from req.body with no schema — no email-format check, no min/max password length. This is inconsistent with the zod validation used on the payments endpoints and (once a real hasher is added) the missing max length is a DoS vector. Add a zod schema at the boundary: z.string().email() and z.string().min(8).max(128).

findings.title: Global JSON body limit raised 80x (64kb -> 5mb)
findings.message: Replacing the 64kb limit with 5mb on the global middleware means every route — /charge, /auth/register, /auth/login — now parses up to 5MB per request, a cheap memory/CPU DoS amplifier. Keep the global limit at 64kb and scope the larger limit to only the route that needs it, e.g. router.get('/admin/export.csv', express.json({ limit: '5mb' }), ...), documenting the payload size that justified it.

findings.title: CSV export buffers the whole table; admin list endpoints have no pagination
findings.message: exportTransactionsCsv calls findAll(), maps to a second array, then join()s into one string — three in-memory copies before any bytes are sent, an OOM risk at scale. Stream rows in batches with res.write() instead of buffering. Relatedly, listUsers/listTransactions/exportCsv accept no limit/offset/cursor, so each reads the full table. Add pagination to the list endpoints and streaming to the export before this runs against production data volumes.

findings.title: console.log used instead of the project pino logger (leaks payloads/emails)
findings.message: webhooksService (lines 32/42/44) logs full serialized webhook payloads via console.log, and userStore.findUserByEmail (line 81) console.logs the raw SQL string including the user's email. Both bypass pino's log-level control and redaction allowlist. Use the injected logger with structured fields (logger.debug/info/error) so sensitive data is redacted and levels are respected.

findings.title: UserRow uses snake_case against a camelCase codebase
findings.message: Transaction and Webhook use camelCase (createdAt, cardLast4) but UserRow exposes password_hash and created_at, and those names leak into the register response. The snake_case appears cargo-culted from the fake SQL layer. Rename to passwordHash/createdAt for consistency.

findings.title: Auth tests only assert token is defined; no negative paths covered
findings.message: Both tests assert res.body.token is truthy but never validate the token, the user shape, or status codes meaningfully. There are no tests for duplicate-email (409), wrong password (401), missing fields (400), the adminBypass/master-key bypasses, or the password_hash leak — so the suite cannot catch any of those regressions. Add response-shape assertions and at least one negative path per branch.
